nativeRange.habitat: Shallow lagoon and seaward reefs at 0–16 m depth, apparently endemic to Tonga

diet.notes: Has been reported to accept finely chopped crustacean flesh, mysis shrimp, vitamin-enriched brine shrimp, and frozen herbivore preparations. Generally advised to feed small portions 2–3 times daily; supplementing with marine algae or spirulina has been recommended.

summary: A striking venomous fang blenny apparently endemic to Tonga, generally considered reef-safe and well suited to established small to medium saltwater aquariums with abundant live rock. Noted for its distinctive hovering behavior and bright green head with a black-bordered dorsal fin.
careNotes:
The Green Canary Blenny is generally considered hardy and suitable for intermediate marine keepers, though it tends to require a mature, well-established tank with abundant live rock for grazing and shelter. It possesses small venomous canine teeth used defensively — bites from hand-feeding or perceived threats have been reported as painful, and the species has been known to bite the inside of an attacker's mouth, deterring predation. Hand-feeding is best avoided, and tanks with curious children should be sited or covered carefully.

The species has been reported to be a capable jumper, so a tight-fitting lid is strongly advised. It tends to occupy mid-water more than typical bottom-dwelling blennies, exhibiting a notable hovering-and-darting swimming style.

Best kept singly or as a mated pair; have been known to be territorial toward conspecifics and similarly shaped fang blennies. Most sources describe them as generally peaceful toward unrelated species and compatible with corals and invertebrates — one source (Dr. Reefs) characterised them as outright peaceful and suitable for groups, while LiveAquaria and SaltCorner described them as semi-aggressive; the intermediate semi-aggressive value is used here.

Sources disagreed on adult size: LiveAquaria, SaltCorner and stlmarinesolutions list 5 inches (12.7 cm), Dr. Reefs lists 4.5 inches (11.4 cm), and FishBase records 10.0 cm SL (apparently maximum standard length). The averaged range of 10–12 cm TL is used here. Care-level guidance also ranged from beginner (Dr. Reefs) to moderate/intermediate (LiveAquaria, SaltCorner); intermediate is used as the middle value.

Males tend to be larger than females and have been observed to undergo color changes during courtship and spawning.
breedingNotes: Has been reported to spawn in captivity in the manner typical of Meiacanthus blennies, with males larger than females and displaying intensified coloration and color shifts during courtship. Eggs are demersal and adhesive; males have been observed to guard clutches. Larvae have been reported to require live rotifers as initial food, making captive rearing moderately challenging.

additional.name: drreefsquarantinedfish.com
additional.notes: Cross-checked water parameters (22–26°C, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.020–1.025), adult size (4.5"/11.4 cm), care level (beginner — disagreed with LiveAquaria's moderate, averaged to intermediate), temperament (peaceful — disagreed with LiveAquaria's semi-aggressive, averaged to semi-aggressive), and lifespan (4–6 years). Confirmed jumping risk and venomous fangs.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Blenniidae, Blenniiformes), maximum length (10.0 cm SL), native range (endemic to Tonga), and habitat depth (0–16 m). Used in adult-size averaging.

additional.name: stlmarinesolutions.com
additional.notes: Third-party retailer cross-check: confirmed 5"/12.7 cm adult size, 30-gallon minimum tank, semi-aggressive temperament, reef-safe yes, omnivore diet. Used in size and temperament averaging.

fish.breedingNotes: Has been documented spawning in captivity with male guarding behavior; larvae require live rotifers as first food, making successful rearing challenging.
